_purpose: Canonical list of mandatory evaluation-pass checks, one entry per lettered sub-check, grouped by report dimension. references/evaluation-rubric.md (agent-facing prose) and validate_findings.py (enforcement) both read this file so the checklist and the validator cannot drift apart. Each check exists once and gets exactly one coverage record per run: status in {risk, strength, clean, not-applicable, not-assessed}. There is no minimum or maximum finding count anywhere in this file or in how it's used — that model was replaced by mandatory check coverage.

checks.summary: Stated target throughput/QPS/TPS or latency/response-time SLO — absent is a risk on its own.
Stated growth horizon or expected-scale-in-N-years figure — absent is a second, distinct risk, not a duplicate of (a).
Carry forward whatever was found (or its absence) into the scalability and performance-cost checks — cite the real number, or note explicitly that severity there couldn't be precisely calibrated.
Named future tenants/integrations/use cases and any stated timeline — absent is a risk.

Stated bar for how fast/cheaply a new instance of the system's core extension point must be addable — if found, carry it into the extensibility check and judge actual extension cost against it directly. Absent is a risk, same as (a), and a distinct fact from it — not a duplicate.
Find and read the vision/strategy doc(s) — empty/missing is a risk on its own.
Check 2-3 concrete, currently in-flight or recently-made architecture decisions against what the vision claims or should claim.
Identity/PK consistency — does the ORM's @Id match the real DDL key?
Normalization/naming — categorize columns; naming-convention shifts or near-duplicate clusters are historical evidence of ad hoc growth.
Referential-integrity strategy — grep FOREIGN KEY/REFERENCES across all DDL files, not just one table's.
Partitioning/sharding maintenance — grep for the actual maintenance job before assuming automation exists; check the partition list's date range against today.
Type choices — decimal vs float, currency handling.
Forward-readiness — check the schema against whatever the product vision names as coming next.
Shadow/parallel representations — does any table store a JSON-blob or otherwise schemaless duplicate of an entity that's normally columnar elsewhere?

Grep config files for every hardcoded capacity number (thread/worker/concurrency counts, timeouts, batch sizes, partition/date horizons) — list all of them, don't stop after the first; compare env-overridable vs. literal; note which sit on the highest-traffic path.

For every expensive external call (data-warehouse queries, third-party APIs, anything that scales with data scanned), check for a cost/rate guardrail and compare it against how a similar call elsewhere in the same codebase is guarded.

Use the repo's own "how to add X" pattern docs (if any) as a test — do the described steps still match reality, and what fraction of actual instances of X follow the pattern vs. duplicate logic inline (exact count, not an estimate)?
Look for the same shape of inconsistency in places with no pattern doc at all — two conceptually-identical concerns modeled two structurally different ways elsewhere in the codebase.
Pick the 2-3 most critical logic paths (highest business impact and/or highest churn) and check all four golden signals — traffic, errors, latency, saturation — separately for each.
Trace whatever code path is responsible for recovering from/reporting on a known failure mode all the way to its output, and check whether that output is a metric or just a log line.

Use compute_churn.py's bus_factor_hotspots/high_diversity_hotspots — cross-reference any single-author high-churn package against what that code actually does; sanity-check with git log --no-merges before citing it.

Check the pattern-doc adoption rate from extensibility.a again here, specifically for what inconsistent adoption costs going forward — a distinct finding from extensibility.a's existence-of-inconsistency one.

For the system's write/mutation paths (consumers, POST/PUT endpoints, scheduled jobs), is retry-on-failure paired with idempotency (idempotency key, unique constraint, upsert) — or would a retry double-process?
Failure isolation — does a downstream dependency failure (DB, external API, broker) have a timeout/circuit-breaker/bulkhead, or can it cascade and take down the caller?

Poison-message/bad-input handling — for consumers/queues, what happens to a message that can never be processed: infinite retry loop, DLQ, or silent drop? Check the actual failure-strategy config, not just its existence.

Transaction boundaries / partial-failure handling — for a multi-step operation (e.g. DB write + event publish), what happens if one step succeeds and the next fails? Look for an outbox pattern, compensating action, or an undocumented inconsistency window.

Recovery expectations — is there a stated or implied recovery point/time objective, and does the actual recovery mechanism (backup, replay, dead-letter reprocessing) match what the system's criticality would require?
API/event schema versioning — are breaking changes versioned, and is there a stated deprecation policy for the old version?

Database migration pattern — evidence of expand/contract (nullable column, backfill, then enforce) vs. a live breaking change (drop/rename column in place)? Check actual migration files/history, not a doc's description of policy.
Consumer/producer compatibility — do consumers tolerate new/unknown fields (forward compatibility), and do producers avoid removing fields a consumer still reads?

Rollback safety — if a recent deploy needed to be rolled back, would the accompanying schema/config/feature change roll back cleanly, or does it create a one-way door (irreversible migration, deleted data, unread-by-old-code new field)?
Gradual-rollout mechanism — for a risky change, is there a feature flag / canary / percentage-rollout path, or is every deploy all-or-nothing?

AuthN/authZ ownership — for each externally-reachable entry point, is there a clear, consistently-applied auth mechanism, or are some endpoints unprotected/inconsistently protected relative to siblings?
Secret handling — grep for hardcoded/committed credentials, API keys, tokens; confirm real secrets come from a secrets manager/env var, not a checked-in default.
Sensitive-data flow — does PII/financial data get logged, cached, or passed to third-party/observability tooling without apparent redaction?

Trust-boundary input validation — for data crossing a trust boundary (user input, external API response, another team's message), is it validated, or trusted implicitly (raw SQL construction, untrusted deserialization)?

Privileged operations — are destructive/high-privilege actions (delete, bulk update, admin operations) gated behind extra checks (confirmation, audit log, elevated permission), or handled the same as routine operations?

system_type_overrides._note: Sparse overrides only. Any (system_type, dimension) pair not listed here defaults to 'mandatory' — including every dimension for production-service, and every dimension for any system_type not listed below at all (e.g. an unclassified or low-confidence repo). This is deliberate: uncertainty about what a repo is defaults to the strictest tier, not the most lenient one. production-service's entry below is deliberately present as an empty object {} rather than omitted — it means the same thing as 'key absent' (everything mandatory), spelled out explicitly for the single most common case so a reader doesn't have to infer it from absence. batch-job's {} means the same thing, for the same reason.
